title: Noun Gender
situation: Every Hindi noun is either masculine or feminine — and every adjective, verb, and possessive must agree. Master this and the whole language clicks.

phrases.hindi: yeh kamra bada hai, yeh kursi chhoti hai
phrases.english: This room is big, this chair is small
phrases.context: The cleanest illustration: kamra (-aa ending) is masculine, kursi (-ii ending) is feminine. Both adjectives shift to match — bada (masc) and chhoti (fem). Memorize this pair as your anchor.
phrases.pronunciation: YEH KAM-ra ba-DA hai, YEH KUR-si chhO-ti hai

phrases.hindi: mera bhai accha hai, meri behen acchi hai
phrases.english: My brother is good, my sister is good
phrases.context: Watch the adjective AND the possessive both change. Three things flip together: mera→meri, accha→acchi.
phrases.pronunciation: ME-ra BHAI ac-CHA hai, ME-ri BE-hen ac-CHI hai

phrases.hindi: yeh dukaan badi hai, yeh bazaar bada hai
phrases.english: This shop is big, this market is big
phrases.context: Dukaan (shop) is feminine despite ending in a consonant — a classic non-obvious feminine. Bazaar is masculine. Watch the adjective flip: badi (fem) vs bada (masc) for the same meaning.
phrases.pronunciation: YEH du-KAAN ba-DI hai, YEH ba-ZAAR ba-DA hai

phrases.hindi: paani saaf hai, chai meethi hai
phrases.english: The water is clean, the tea is sweet
phrases.context: Paani (water) is masculine even though it ends in -ii — a classic exception. Chai is feminine. Watch: 'saaf' never changes (invariable adjective), but 'meetha' shifts to 'meethi' because chai is feminine.
phrases.pronunciation: paa-NI SAAF hai, CHAI mee-THI hai

phrases.hindi: darwaza khul gaya, khidki khul gayi
phrases.english: The door opened, the window opened
phrases.context: Watch the past verb: gaya (masc, agrees with darwaza) vs gayi (fem, agrees with khidki). Verb gender is non-negotiable in past tense — same action, different verb ending because the subject gender differs.
phrases.pronunciation: dar-WAA-za khul ga-YA, khid-KI khul ga-YI

phrases.hindi: mere paas do bhai aur ek behen hai
phrases.english: I have two brothers and one sister
phrases.context: When mixing masc + fem in a list, the verb agrees with the nearest noun. Here 'behen' (fem) is nearest, but the older convention takes masculine — both are heard. Don't overthink.
phrases.pronunciation: ME-re paas DO BHAI aur EK BE-hen hai

phrases.hindi: Hindi mushkil hai par interesting hai
phrases.context: Hindi (the language) is feminine. 'Mushkil' is invariable — adjectives ending in non-aa don't change. So 'Hindi mushkil hai' (no change to mushkil).
phrases.pronunciation: HIN-di mush-KIL hai par IN-te-res-ting hai

phrases.hindi: yeh aurat doctor hai, woh aadmi engineer hai
phrases.english: This woman is a doctor, that man is an engineer
phrases.context: Aurat (woman) is feminine, aadmi (man) is masculine despite ending in -ii. Profession words (doctor, engineer, teacher) typically don't show gender in modern usage.
phrases.pronunciation: YEH au-RAT DOC-tor hai, WOH aad-MI EN-gi-neer hai

phrases.hindi: meri gaadi nayi hai, mera scooter purana hai
phrases.english: My car is new, my scooter is old
phrases.context: Gaadi (vehicle/car) is feminine — typical -ii ending. Scooter is masculine (English loanword defaulting to masc). Pattern: native words mostly follow -aa/-ii rule, loanwords mostly masc.
phrases.pronunciation: ME-ri GAA-di na-YI hai, ME-ra SCOO-ter pu-RAA-na hai

phrases.hindi: acche dost milte hain mushkil se
phrases.english: Good friends are hard to come by
phrases.context: Plural masc: accha → acche, dost (friend) stays same. 'milte hain' = are found. Watch all three masculine markers: acche, dost (with plural verb), milte.
phrases.pronunciation: ac-CHE DOST mil-TE hain mush-KIL se

grammar_notes: Hindi has TWO genders — masculine (pulling) and feminine (stri-ling). There is no neuter. Every single noun has a gender — including abstract ideas (pyaar = love, masc; khushi = happiness, fem)
Default rule #1: nouns ending in -aa are usually masculine (ladka, kamra, raasta, samosa). Default rule #2: nouns ending in -ii are usually feminine (ladki, kursi, gaadi, biwi)
Major exceptions to memorize: paani (water-M), aadmi (man-M), ghee (M) — end in -ii but masculine. Aurat (woman-F), kitaab (book-F), cheez (thing-F), raat (night-F) — end in consonant but feminine

Adjectives ending in -aa change with gender: accha → acchi → acche. Adjectives NOT ending in -aa never change: saaf (clean), mushkil (hard), khoobsoorat (beautiful), bekaar (useless) — same for all genders

Possessives ALWAYS agree with the OWNED noun, not the owner: mera bhai (my brother — owned thing is masc), meri behen (my sister — owned thing is fem), mere parents (my parents — plural). Same for tera/tumhara/uska/hamara
Past tense verbs agree with subject in intransitive sentences (ladka aaya / ladki aayi) and with object in 'ne'-marked transitive sentences (usne kitaab padhi / usne phone uthaya)
Loanwords from English usually default to masculine: phone, computer, scooter, table. Some have become feminine through usage: bus, car, train (because they replaced feminine native words like gaadi)
culture_notes: Native speakers don't 'think about' gender — it's automatic, like 'he/she/it' in English. As a learner, mistakes are forgivable but they'll mark you as a beginner
Some words have unstable gender depending on region — 'mobile' is usually masc in Delhi, sometimes fem in Lucknow. Don't stress, just pick one and stay consistent
When unsure, default to masculine — it's the unmarked gender and you'll be wrong less than 50% of the time on borrowed words

skill_breakdown.explanation: Start with the -aa/-ii rule for native words. For nouns ending in consonants, memorize them as you encounter them. For loanwords, default to masculine unless it's clearly replacing a feminine native word. Build your gender intuition by always learning new nouns WITH a gender-revealing adjective.

more_examples.english: this is a table (fem despite consonant ending)
this is a road (fem despite consonant)

skill_breakdown.explanation: Only adjectives ending in -aa change. The change: -aa for masc singular, -ii for feminine, -e for masc plural or before 'aap'. Adjectives ending in consonants or other vowels (saaf, mushkil, bekaar, khoobsoorat) NEVER change. Quick test: does the adjective end in -aa? If yes, it agrees. If no, it doesn't.

more_examples.hindi: bada kamra / badi kitaab / bade ghar
more_examples.english: big room / big book / big houses
clean room / clean book (saaf never changes)

more_examples.hindi: naya phone / nayi gaadi / naye joote
more_examples.english: new phone / new car / new shoes

skill_breakdown.explanation: In present tense, verbs agree with subject: karta (M-sg), karti (F-sg), karte (M-pl/respectful). In past tense, intransitive verbs agree with subject (woh aaya/aayi), but transitive verbs with 'ne' agree with the OBJECT (usne kitaab padhi — padhi because kitaab is fem). This 'ne' rule is the trickiest part of Hindi grammar.

more_examples.english: the boy eats food (present, masc subject)
the girl eats food (present, fem subject)
the boy came, the girl came (past, intransitive)
he/she ate roti (ne + fem object → khayi)
he/she ate food (ne + masc object → khaya)

practice_prompt: You are a Hindi tutor drilling noun gender — agreement ripples through adjectives, verbs (especially past with `ne`), and demonstratives, so hammer it from three angles. Use `aap`. Open with `chaliye noun gender practice karte hain — pehla: 'kitaab' masculine hai ya feminine?` and mix 6-8 items across three drill modes: (a) gender-quiz — name a noun, aap bataiye m/f; (b) agreement-fix — give a wrong-agreement sentence like `meri bhai accha hai` and ask aap to correct; (c) translation — `she is good` → user supplies `woh acchi hai` with the right match. Escalate from simple m/f calls (ladka, gaadi) to exception nouns (aadmi-m, kitaab-f, paani-m), then full agreement (mera/meri/mere, accha/acchi/acche), then preview past-tense object agreement with `ne` (`usne roti khayi` vs `usne khaana khaya`). Watch for: wrong call on exceptions, missed adjective shift (-aa→-i for f), and English-style 'is' carrying gender (Hindi `hai` never changes). Flag mistakes as `[[CORRECTION: original="..." correct="..." reason="..."]]`. Don't switch to English unless asked. End with `acche se yaad rakhiye — har naye shabd ke saath uska gender bhi yaad karein. phir milte hain`.
references: Snell & Weightman, 'Teach Yourself Hindi' — Ch. 1-2 on noun gender and adjective agreement
McGregor, 'Outline of Hindi Grammar' — Sections on gender, oblique case, and the 'ne' construction
Afroz Taj, 'A Door Into Hindi' (UNC) — Lesson 2 introduces gender systematically with audio examples

theory.intro: Hindi has two genders — masculine and feminine — and every single noun has one. Get the gender wrong and the whole sentence breaks: adjectives, verbs, even some postpositions change to match the noun's gender. There's no neuter and no 'just memorize the article' shortcut — gender lives inside the word itself and ripples outward to everything connected to it. Master this and a huge amount of Hindi clicks into place.

sections.heading: The two genders
sections.body:
Every Hindi noun is either **masculine** or **feminine**. There is no neuter and no third option.

When you learn a new noun, learn its gender at the same time — write it as `ladka (m)` or `kitaab (f)`. That habit pays off everywhere.

Gender controls more than English prepares you for. Compare these two sentences — same meaning, different gender, almost every word changes:

callout.body: Notice that the verb `hai` stays the same — it's only the adjective that shifts. We'll see when verbs shift too in the section below.

sections.cutting_intro: Hi! Let's start simple. Every Hindi noun has a gender — and it changes everything else in the sentence. Watch.

quick_check.question: How many grammatical genders does Hindi have?
quick_check.explanation: Hindi has only two — masculine and feminine. No neuter at all.

sections.heading: Spot the pattern from the ending
sections.body:
Most Hindi nouns hint at their gender through their ending. Use it as your **first guess** — right about 75% of the time.

- ending in `-aa` → usually masculine: `ladka`, `kamra`, `raasta`
- ending in `-ii` / `-i` → usually feminine: `ladki`, `kursi`, `roti`, `gaadi`
- ending in `-aa` (Sanskrit-origin) → feminine: `bhasha`, `seva`, `katha`
- irregular → just memorize: `aadmi` (m), `aurat` (f), `kitaab` (f), `raat` (f), `paani` (m)

Learn the exceptions as you meet them — they're common everyday words, so they come up fast.

table.caption: Common noun-ending patterns
rows: aadmi (m), aurat (f), haath (m), kitaab (f), raat (f), paani (m)

callout.body: Pattern is a guide, not a rule. Common everyday nouns break it — `aadmi` (man) ends in `-i` but is masculine; `kitaab` (book) ends in a consonant but is feminine. Always learn the gender alongside the word.

sections.cutting_intro: Now — most nouns hint at their gender through their ending. Pattern works about 75% of the time. Let's see it.

quick_check.question: A noun ending in `-i` like `ladki` or `kursi` is usually...
quick_check.explanation: Yes — `-i` / `-ii` ending = usually feminine. Patterns aren't 100% (aadmi is the famous exception) but it's your best first guess.

sections.heading: Adjective agreement
sections.body:
Adjectives split into two camps: those that change with gender, and those that don't. The rule is mechanical: **does the adjective end in `-aa`?**

- ends in `-aa` → it **shifts** to match the noun:
  - `-aa` → masculine singular: `accha khaana`
  - `-i` → feminine: `acchi chai`
  - `-e` → masculine plural: `acche dost`
- does NOT end in `-aa` → **never changes**: `khush`, `laal`, `theek`, `mushkil`, `khoobsoorat`

So `laal kapda` and `laal saari` are both just `laal` — no adjustment needed.

examples.breakdown: laal doesn't end in -aa, so no change regardless of gender

callout.body: Hard rule: -aa adjectives always shift to match the noun. The shift is mechanical: -aa → -i for feminine, -aa → -e for masculine plural. Memorize this transform; you'll use it constantly.

sections.cutting_intro: Here's where it ripples outward. Adjectives ending in -aa change to match the noun's gender. Like clothes, the adjective dresses up to match.

quick_check.question: Which adjective form goes with `chai` (feminine)?
quick_check.explanation: Correct! `chai` is feminine, so the -aa adjective shifts to -i. acchi chai = good tea.

sections.heading: Verb agreement (the harder bit)
sections.body:
Verb agreement has two patterns depending on tense.

**Present tense** — the verb agrees with the **subject's** gender:
- `main jaa raha hoon` — masculine speaker
- `main jaa rahi hoon` — feminine speaker

Same sentence, same meaning, different verb ending because the speaker's gender changed.

**Past tense with `ne`** (the ergative-past construction) — the verb agrees with the **object's** gender instead:
- `maine roti khayi` — `roti` is feminine → verb ends in `-i`
- `maine khaana khaya` — `khaana` is masculine → verb ends in `-a`

The subject `main` hasn't changed — the verb flipped because the object did. This catches every English speaker off guard.

examples.breakdown: subject = main (1st person, here masc.); verb ending matches
subject = main (1st person, here fem.); verb ending matches
object = roti (f); verb ending = -i (feminine), regardless of speaker gender
object = khaana (m); verb ending = -a (masculine), regardless of speaker gender

callout.body: The `ne` rule has its own foundation lesson. For now just notice: past-tense verbs with `ne` track the OBJECT's gender. Drill this; it's one of the top three things English speakers get wrong in Hindi.

sections.cutting_intro: Heads up — this one trips up every English speaker. In some past-tense constructions, the VERB tracks the OBJECT's gender, not the subject's.

quick_check.question: `Maine roti khayi.` Why does the verb end in `-i`?
options: It's just how past tense works
quick_check.explanation: Yes! With `ne`, the verb agrees with the OBJECT's gender. `roti` is feminine, so the verb ends in -i. The speaker could be any gender — it doesn't matter.

sections.heading: Gendering English loanwords
sections.body:
Hindi borrows English nouns constantly and assigns gender by tracking a similar Hindi word. The patterns aren't strict rules, but they're **consistent enough to guess from**:

- tech and machines → masculine, tracking `yantra` (machine, m)
- vehicles → feminine, tracking `gaadi` (vehicle, f)
- foods and drinks → feminine, tracking `chai` (f)
- everything else → lean masculine (it's the unmarked default)

When you hear a native speaker use a gender for a loanword, just adopt it and move on — these are stable in everyday use.

table.caption: Common English loanwords and their adopted gender
rows: no clear tracker — defaults masc (`email aaya` = the email came-masc.)

callout.body: When in doubt, listen to which gender locals use — and lean masculine for tech/objects, feminine for foods/vehicles. Native speakers don't always agree on edge cases, so don't stress; pick one and be consistent.

sections.cutting_intro: Real-life Hindi mixes English words all the time. Speakers assign gender on instinct, tracking a similar Hindi noun.

quick_check.question: What gender does Hindi typically assign to the English word `car`?
quick_check.explanation: Feminine — it tracks `gaadi` (vehicle, feminine). Same for `bus` and other vehicles. Tech words like computer / mobile go masculine instead.

theory.wrap_up: Gender isn't optional in Hindi — it's structural. The single most useful habit is to learn the gender WITH the noun, every time you encounter a new word. Write it like a flashcard: `kitaab (f) — book`, `aadmi (m) — man`. The phrases below give you practice spotting and applying gender in real conversations. Pay attention to adjective endings as you read them.
